findings.description: Email/SMTP header injection (CWE-93/CRLF): _render interpolates unvalidated msg.to and msg.subject directly into CRLF-delimited headers, so a value containing \r\n can inject arbitrary headers (e.g. Bcc) or rewrite the body.
msg.to is placed raw into the 'To:' header line with \r\n separators, allowing recipient/header spoofing via CRLF injection in the address field.
msg.subject is placed raw into the 'Subject:' header line, the most attacker-controllable field, enabling header injection and body rewrite via embedded \r\n.

sendmail uses unvalidated msg.to as the SMTP envelope recipient and the injection-prone _render output as the message, so malicious to/subject values manipulate the actual mail transaction and delivery.

Cleartext, unauthenticated SMTP submission (CWE-319): connects on the STARTTLS submission port 587 but never calls starttls() or login(), so message bodies are transmitted in plaintext and exposed to interception/MITM.

smtplib.SMTP() is constructed outside the try/finally, so a connection failure propagates out of flush, leaves _pending uncleared, and aborts sending all remaining queued messages (availability/connection-leak, CWE-404).

Dedup key is msg.to alone (ignores subject/body), so a benign pre-enqueued message to an address silently suppresses a later distinct message (e.g. a security/reset notification) to the same recipient (denial-of-notification, CWE-697).
_seen grows unbounded for the lifetime of the queue (only cleared via clear_dedup_cache), allowing slow memory growth under sustained enqueueing.
